title: Afghanistan–Pakistan Eid al-Fitr Ceasefire Takes Effect
detail: A five-day Eid al-Fitr ceasefire between Pakistan and Afghanistan came into effect at midnight on March 19, brokered at the request of Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Turkey. It is the first cessation of hostilities since Pakistan declared 'open war' against Afghanistan following renewed clashes in February. Pakistan's Information Minister Tarar said the ceasefire would run through midnight on March 24, offering the gesture 'in good faith and in keeping with Islamic norms,' but warned operations would resume immediately if any cross-border attack or terrorist incident occurred inside Pakistan. Afghan spokesperson Zabiullah Mujahid confirmed the pause, though without specifying a timeframe. The truce followed a mass funeral in Kabul on March 18 for victims of the March 16 Pakistani airstrike on the Omid drug rehabilitation center, which Afghan authorities said killed at least 408 people.

media.caption: Afghanistan-Pakistan Eid al-Fitr ceasefire takes effect amid mass funeral in Kabul

title: TTP Announces 3-Day Eid Ceasefire Inside Pakistan
detail: The Pakistani Taliban (Tehrik-e-Taliban Pakistan, TTP) announced a three-day Eid ceasefire, hours after Pakistan and Afghanistan declared their own temporary pause in fighting. TTP spokesman Mohammad Khurasani said the ceasefire was intended to allow people to observe Eid al-Fitr, with the truce set to take effect from the first day of Eid subject to a moon sighting. The TTP, while separate from but allied to the Afghan Taliban, has stepped up attacks inside Pakistan since the Afghan Taliban returned to power in August 2021. The simultaneous ceasefire announcements were seen as a coordinated de-escalation driven by Gulf and Turkish mediation.

title: UN: 115,000 Afghans Displaced, 289 Civilians Killed Since Feb 26
detail: As the Eid ceasefire took effect, the UN released updated figures on the humanitarian toll of the Afghanistan–Pakistan conflict: approximately 115,000 Afghans had been displaced since hostilities intensified on February 26, and at least 289 civilians had been killed in Afghanistan. The UN refugee agency warned of rapidly worsening conditions in eastern and southeastern provinces. WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said the intensifying conflict was 'placing additional strain on health systems' and noted that at least six health facilities in Afghanistan had been affected by the violence since late February. The ongoing conflict in the Middle East was also disrupting Afghanistan's trade routes through Iran, adding further economic pressure.
